content.text: Running an LLM locally is fundamentally a hardware problem: the model's weights must fit into fast memory, inference kernels need computational throughput, and the whole stack must be assembled from GPU, RAM, CPU, and storage that work together. Understanding these requirements lets you choose the right model size, avoid silent CPU-fallback slow-downs, and buy hardware that gives useful tokens-per-second for real agent workloads.
What it is

Hardware requirements for local LLM inference describe the minimum and recommended specifications for GPU VRAM, system RAM, CPU, storage, and PCIe bandwidth needed to load and run a given model. The dominant constraint is almost always GPU VRAM: the entire set of model weights, plus the key-value cache for the current context, must reside in VRAM for GPU-accelerated inference. When weights spill into system RAM or disk, every forward pass involves slow PCIe transfers that cut tokens-per-second by 10x or more.

The four major hardware resources and their roles are: GPU VRAM (weights + KV-cache, the primary bottleneck), system RAM (CPU offload layers, OS, runtime), CPU (tokenizer, pre/post-processing, and pure-CPU inference fallback), and NVMe SSD (model file storage; only matters at load time once model is in VRAM).
Why it matters

Mismatched hardware is the most common reason local inference fails or performs unusably. A developer who downloads a 70B model onto a 16 GB GPU discovers at runtime that weights spill to RAM, producing 1-2 tokens/sec instead of the expected 20-40 tokens/sec. Understanding VRAM budgeting before purchasing or downloading prevents wasted time and money. For agent developers specifically, tokens-per-second directly affects loop latency: an agent that calls a local model 10 times per task at 5 tokens/sec spends minutes on inference that should take seconds.
Benefits
Privacy and data residency: all inference happens on-machine; no tokens leave the building, which is essential for confidential documents, medical data, or customer PII.

Zero marginal cost: once hardware is purchased, inference runs at the cost of electricity (roughly $0.002-$0.01 per 1 M tokens on a mid-range GPU), orders of magnitude cheaper than cloud API rates for high-volume workloads.
Offline capability: air-gapped environments, laptops on airplanes, factory floors without internet connectivity — local inference works everywhere.
Reproducibility: pinning a model file and runtime version produces deterministic outputs across time, unlike cloud APIs where model versions change silently.
Low latency for first token: local inference on a warm GPU eliminates network round-trip overhead, giving first-token latency under 100 ms on adequately provisioned hardware.
Fine-tuned model deployment: GGUF and safetensors files for custom fine-tuned models can be dropped into the same inference stack without cloud model registry overhead.
Trade-offs
High upfront hardware cost: a workstation GPU with 24 GB VRAM (e.g. RTX 4090) costs $1,500-$2,000; the NVIDIA A100 80 GB needed for unquantized 70B costs $10,000+ used.
Power consumption: a GPU under sustained load draws 250-700 W; cooling and electricity add to total cost of ownership, especially in warm climates.
Capability ceiling: even quantized 70B models underperform frontier cloud models on complex reasoning; local inference is not a quality substitute for every task.
Maintenance burden: CUDA driver updates, runtime version compatibility, model format migrations (GGUF versions, tensor layouts) require ongoing attention.
Single-user throughput: a consumer GPU serves one request at a time or a small batch; cloud inference scales horizontally without any per-user hardware.
How it works

At inference time, a transformer LLM is essentially a sequence of matrix multiplications. Each matrix (a weight tensor) lives in VRAM as a block of floating-point or integer values. The GPU executes thousands of parallel CUDA cores against these tensors, producing logits that become the next token. The memory consumed by weights is straightforward to calculate:
VRAM for weights (bytes) = parameter_count x bytes_per_parameter. For FP16 (2 bytes): a 7B model needs 7,000,000,000 x 2 = 14 GB. For INT4 (0.5 bytes effective): the same 7B model needs about 3.5 GB.

On top of weight memory, the KV-cache holds past attention keys and values for the current context window. KV-cache size scales with batch size, context length, number of attention heads, and head dimension. A rough formula for one sequence at full context:

KV-cache (bytes) = 2 x num_layers x num_heads x head_dim x context_length x bytes_per_element. For Llama-3.1-8B at 8192 context in FP16: 2 x 32 x 8 x 128 x 8192 x 2 = approx 1.07 GB. This grows linearly with context length.

Memory bandwidth, not raw FLOPS, limits generation throughput for single-stream inference. Each new token reads all weights from VRAM once. An RTX 4090 has 1,008 GB/s bandwidth; at FP16 a 7B model (14 GB) can be read in about 14 ms, giving a theoretical ceiling of roughly 70 tokens/sec. Practical rates are 40-60 tokens/sec because of memory access patterns and CUDA overhead.
Architecture

The diagram below shows the four hardware layers and the VRAM budget for common model sizes. Arrows indicate the dependency flow: model files load from SSD into VRAM via PCIe; the CPU handles pre/post-processing and can offload layers when VRAM is insufficient.
When to use it
Local inference is the right choice when one or more of the following conditions hold:
Data cannot leave the premises: healthcare records, legal documents, source code under NDA, financial models with IP restrictions.
Volume is high enough that API cost exceeds hardware amortisation: rule of thumb is 200+ million tokens/month where local hardware pays back within 6 months.
Network is unavailable or unreliable: edge devices, embedded systems, military or industrial environments.
A fine-tuned model must be served: GGUF and safetensors fine-tuned weights run identically on local runtimes without cloud model-hosting overhead.
Development and testing only: local 7B or 8B quant runs free and offline for iterating on agent prompts, tool-calling schemas, and orchestration logic before touching paid APIs.

Cloud inference is better when the task requires a frontier model capability (complex multi-step reasoning, vision, very long context), when concurrent user volume is highly variable, or when capital budget for hardware is not available.
How to implement

The first step is to audit available hardware and calculate whether your target model fits. The following Python script checks VRAM availability and estimates fit for a list of model sizes and quantization levels.

"""hardware_check.py -- Inventory VRAM and estimate which models fit."""
import subprocess

# Bytes per parameter for each precision format
BYTES_PER_PARAM = {
    "fp32": 4,
    "fp16": 2,
    "bf16": 2,
    "int8": 1,
    "int4": 0.5,   # 4-bit quantisation (GGUF Q4_K_M etc.)
    "int3": 0.375,
    "int2": 0.25,
}

KV_OVERHEAD_GB = 1.5   # rough KV-cache for 8k context, single sequence
SYSTEM_OVERHEAD_GB = 1.0  # CUDA context, activations


def get_gpu_vram_gb() -> list:
    """Return list of (gpu_name, vram_gb) for all NVIDIA GPUs."""
    try:
        out = subprocess.check_output(
            ["nvidia-smi",
             "--query-gpu=name,memory.total",
             "--format=csv,noheader,nounits"],
            text=True,
        )
    except (FileNotFoundError, subprocess.CalledProcessError):
        return []
    results = []
    for line in out.strip().splitlines():
        name, mem_mib = line.split(",")
        results.append((name.strip(), float(mem_mib.strip()) / 1024))
    return results


def vram_needed_gb(params_b: float, precision: str) -> float:
    """Estimate total VRAM in GB for weights + KV-cache + system overhead."""
    weight_gb = params_b * 1e9 * BYTES_PER_PARAM[precision] / 1e9
    return weight_gb + KV_OVERHEAD_GB + SYSTEM_OVERHEAD_GB


def main():
    gpus = get_gpu_vram_gb()
    if not gpus:
        print("No NVIDIA GPU found (or nvidia-smi not on PATH). CPU-only inference only.")
        available_gb = 0.0
    else:
        for name, gb in gpus:
            print(f"  GPU: {name}  VRAM: {gb:.1f} GB")
        available_gb = sum(gb for _, gb in gpus)   # multi-GPU: sum VRAM

    print()
    models = [
        ("7B",  7),
        ("8B",  8),
        ("13B", 13),
        ("34B", 34),
        ("70B", 70),
    ]
    precisions = ["fp16", "int8", "int4"]

    header = f"{'Model':<8}" + "".join(f"{p:>12}" for p in precisions)
    print(header)
    print("-" * (8 + 12 * len(precisions)))
    for name, params in models:
        row = f"{name:<8}"
        for prec in precisions:
            needed = vram_needed_gb(params, prec)
            fits = "OK" if (available_gb > 0 and available_gb >= needed) else "TOO BIG"
            row += f"  {needed:5.1f} GB {fits:>5}"
        print(row)


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()
After confirming VRAM, use Ollama or llama.cpp to load the model and measure actual tokens-per-second. The script below runs a benchmark against a local Ollama endpoint.

"""benchmark_local.py -- Measure tokens/sec from a local Ollama instance."""
import time
import json
import urllib.request

OLLAMA_URL = "http://localhost:11434/api/generate"
MODEL = "llama3.2:3b"          # change to your model tag
PROMPT = "Explain gradient descent in 200 words."


def benchmark(model: str, prompt: str, n_runs: int = 3) -> dict:
    results = []
    for i in range(n_runs):
        payload = json.dumps({"model": model, "prompt": prompt, "stream": False}).encode()
        req = urllib.request.Request(
            OLLAMA_URL,
            data=payload,
            headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
        )
        t0 = time.perf_counter()
        with urllib.request.urlopen(req, timeout=120) as resp:
            body = json.loads(resp.read())
        elapsed = time.perf_counter() - t0

        eval_count = body.get("eval_count", 0)
        eval_duration_ns = body.get("eval_duration", 1)
        tps = eval_count / (eval_duration_ns / 1e9)
        results.append({
            "run": i + 1,
            "tokens": eval_count,
            "tps": round(tps, 1),
            "wall_s": round(elapsed, 2)
        })
        print(f"  run {i + 1}: {eval_count} tokens @ {tps:.1f} tok/s ({elapsed:.1f}s wall)")

    avg_tps = sum(r["tps"] for r in results) / len(results)
    return {"model": model, "avg_tps": round(avg_tps, 1), "runs": results}


if __name__ == "__main__":
    print(f"Benchmarking model: {MODEL}")
    summary = benchmark(MODEL, PROMPT)
    print(f"\nAverage: {summary['avg_tps']} tokens/sec")
Examples
Hardware configurations and what they can run comfortably:
Hardware
VRAM
Best model fit
Typical tok/s
MacBook Pro M3 Pro
18 GB unified
8B INT4 or 13B INT4
24 GB
13B FP16 or 34B INT4
RTX 3090 x2 (NVLink)
48 GB
34B FP16 or 70B INT4
A100 80 GB (cloud or server)
80 GB
70B FP16 or 2x 70B INT4
CPU-only (Ryzen 9 + 64 GB RAM)
0 GB GPU
7B INT4 (slow)
Real-world example

A startup builds a document-review agent for legal contracts. Their requirement is that no contract text may be sent to external services. They have one RTX 4090 (24 GB VRAM) in a workstation. Using the VRAM formula, a Q4_K_M quantised Mistral-7B (3.8 GB) fits easily, leaving 20 GB for KV-cache and a large context window of 32k tokens. They run Ollama, configure the agent to call the local OpenAI-compatible endpoint, and iterate on tool-calling prompts entirely offline. When throughput becomes a bottleneck (the agent processes 200 contracts per day), they add a second RTX 4090 and split-load Mistral-34B-Instruct-Q4 across both cards using llama.cpp's tensor parallelism, doubling throughput while staying within the data-residency requirement.
Mental model

Think of VRAM as a workbench: the model's weights are the tools you lay out before starting. If all the tools fit on the bench, every operation is fast. If some tools must be stored in a nearby cabinet (system RAM) or a distant warehouse (disk), each use of those tools costs a trip that dominates total time. Keep everything on the bench -- fit the model in VRAM -- and the workbench speed (memory bandwidth) determines your pace.
Common mistakes
Ignoring VRAM headroom: downloading a model that fills VRAM exactly leaves no room for KV-cache, causing out-of-memory (OOM) errors when context grows. Always leave 20-25% VRAM free after weights.
Confusing system RAM and VRAM: "I have 64 GB of RAM" does not help if GPU VRAM is only 8 GB. The model still runs at RAM bandwidth (~50 GB/s) rather than GPU bandwidth (~900 GB/s) -- 10-20x slower.
Buying older GPU generations: an RTX 2080 Ti has 11 GB VRAM but memory bandwidth of only 616 GB/s, versus 1,008 GB/s on the RTX 4090. Generation matters as much as VRAM.
Running FP32 unnecessarily: most inference runtimes default to FP16 for modern models. Accidentally enabling FP32 doubles weight memory and halves throughput with no quality gain.
Underestimating storage: a single 70B GGUF Q4_K_M file is ~40 GB. Storing multiple quantisation variants and multiple models quickly requires 1-2 TB of fast NVMe.
Ignoring thermal throttling: a GPU running at 90 degrees C for hours will drop clock speed and reduce tokens/sec. Ensure case airflow or a dedicated server chassis with proper cooling.
Multi-GPU without NVLink: two GPUs communicating over PCIe (not NVLink) share a 64 GB/s bus rather than 900 GB/s NVLink, making multi-GPU tensor parallelism much slower than expected.
Best practices
Budget 80% rule: only load a model that needs at most 80% of your GPU VRAM. The remaining 20% absorbs KV-cache growth and runtime overhead.
Start with INT4 quantisation: Q4_K_M (llama.cpp naming) offers the best quality-per-GB ratio for most LLMs. Only upgrade to INT8 or FP16 if quality degradation is measurable on your task.
Profile before buying: rent a GPU cloud instance (Lambda Labs, RunPod) for $1-2/hour, run your workload, and confirm tokens/sec meets requirements before committing to hardware purchase.

Match context length to KV-cache budget: if you only need 4k context, configure the server for 4k (--ctx-size 4096 in llama.cpp). This saves VRAM that can be redirected to a larger batch size or longer-context fallback.
Enable Flash Attention 2: all major runtimes support FA2, which reduces KV-cache memory from quadratic to linear in practice, making long-context workloads fit in VRAM that would otherwise OOM.
Pin CUDA and driver versions: major CUDA version upgrades can break llama.cpp or vLLM builds. Use Docker images that pin a known-good combination.
Performance & scalability

Generation throughput (tokens/sec) in single-stream inference is dominated by memory bandwidth, not compute. The formula is: theoretical peak tok/s = GPU memory bandwidth (GB/s) / model weight size (GB). For a 7B INT4 model (3.5 GB weights) on an RTX 4090 (1,008 GB/s): 1008 / 3.5 = approximately 288 tokens/sec theoretical; practical is 80-100 tok/s due to overhead.

Batch inference changes the picture: with a batch of 8 requests, the GPU reads weights once but produces 8 output tokens per pass, improving effective throughput 4-6x. vLLM and TGI implement continuous batching to exploit this.

For prefill (processing the prompt), compute is the bottleneck, not bandwidth. Long prompts benefit most from high TFLOPS GPUs. An A100 at 77.6 TFLOPS will prefill a 16k-token prompt 3-4x faster than a consumer GPU with similar memory bandwidth.

Speculative decoding (using a small draft model to propose tokens, verified by the main model) can increase effective tokens/sec by 2-3x with no quality loss when the draft model is well-matched to the distribution. Ollama 0.6+ and vLLM support this.

# Check real-time GPU utilization and memory during inference
nvidia-smi dmon -s mu -d 2
# Output columns: memory used (MiB), GPU utilization (%)
# Aim for GPU util >90% during generation to confirm GPU-bound inference
# If GPU util is <50%, weights may be spilling to system RAM

# Check memory bandwidth utilization with nvtop (install: apt install nvtop)
nvtop

# For Apple Silicon (Metal), check unified memory usage:
sudo powermetrics --samplers gpu_power -n 5 -i 1000

# llama.cpp: set number of GPU layers (ngl) to push all layers to GPU
# --ngl 999 means "put as many layers as possible in GPU"
./llama-server -m ./models/llama-3.2-3b-instruct-q4_k_m.gguf --ngl 999 --ctx-size 8192

# Check how many layers were actually loaded to GPU (look for n_gpu_layers output)
# If n_gpu_layers < model layers, VRAM was insufficient and some layers fell back to CPU
Tooling & ecosystem
Inference runtimes
llama.cpp: C++ implementation with CUDA, Metal, and CPU backends. Widest format support (GGUF Q2-Q8). Reference implementation for quantised local inference.
Ollama: wraps llama.cpp with a daemon, model registry, and OpenAI-compatible HTTP API. Best developer experience for getting started in 5 minutes.
vLLM: Python inference server with PagedAttention for efficient KV-cache management. Best for multi-user or batch workloads; requires NVIDIA CUDA.
LM Studio: GUI application for macOS/Windows that manages model downloads and runs llama.cpp under the hood. No CLI required.
Transformers + bitsandbytes: HuggingFace stack supporting load_in_4bit and load_in_8bit for on-the-fly quantisation of safetensors models without converting to GGUF.
Monitoring and diagnostics
nvidia-smi: built-in VRAM usage, temperature, power draw. Run nvidia-smi dmon for live streaming.
nvtop: htop-style GPU monitor showing per-process VRAM, compute, and memory bandwidth utilisation.
Ollama REST API: GET /api/ps returns currently loaded models and VRAM consumption per model.
torch.cuda.memory_summary(): Python function for detailed CUDA allocator stats when using PyTorch-based runtimes.

Local GPU (owned)
Complete
High-volume, privacy-sensitive

No ownership
$0 (pay/hr)
Depends on provider
Variable load, no hardware budget
CPU-only inference
$0 extra
Complete
Small models, dev/test, no GPU available

None
Data leaves premises

Security & reliability
Security advantages of local inference

No data egress: all prompt text, context, and completions stay on the local machine. There is no network socket opened to a third-party API, which eliminates the risk of man-in-the-middle capture, provider logging, or regulatory data-transfer violations.

Model integrity: once a GGUF or safetensors file is downloaded and checksummed (Ollama uses SHA-256 digests per layer), the exact model weights are pinned. Cloud providers can silently update model weights; local files do not change unless you pull an update.
Air-gap compatibility: local inference works with no internet connection, satisfying the most restrictive air-gapped deployment requirements.
Reliability considerations
Hardware failure is a single point of failure: unlike distributed cloud inference, one GPU dying stops the service. Mitigate with UPS, RAID for model storage, and a cold-standby second machine.

VRAM corruption: GPUs under sustained load can occasionally produce NaN outputs from overheating or memory errors. ECC (Error Correcting Code) VRAM is available on data center GPUs (A100, H100) and prevents silent corruption. Consumer RTX GPUs lack ECC.

Driver and CUDA version drift: over months, OS updates can break CUDA driver compatibility. Pin the NVIDIA driver version in package preferences or use containerized inference that bundles its own CUDA.
Key takeaways
VRAM is the primary constraint: model weights plus KV-cache must fit in GPU VRAM for full-speed inference. Calculate before downloading.
Weight memory formula: param_count_billions x bytes_per_param. FP16 = 2 bytes, INT4 = 0.5 bytes. Add 1.5-2 GB for KV-cache at 8k context.
Bandwidth, not FLOPS, limits single-stream generation: choose GPUs with high memory bandwidth (>900 GB/s) for fast tokens-per-second.
INT4 quantisation is the practical sweet spot: 4-bit GGUF models give 3-5% quality loss while fitting 4x larger models into the same VRAM.
Local inference excels for privacy, reproducibility, and offline operation; cloud APIs excel for frontier capability and elastic scale.
Always benchmark first: rent a matching cloud GPU for a few hours before purchasing hardware to confirm actual tokens-per-second on your real workload.
Apple Silicon unified memory is a practical exception: M-series chips share memory between CPU and GPU, so 64 GB of unified RAM can run INT4 70B models without a discrete GPU.
